content:
9 min read
Just now

--

Part 1 of the Advanced Algorithms & Data Structures Series — algorithms from research papers and production systems that never made it into the classroom.

Imagine you’re three weeks into a systems engineering role. A senior engineer drops a ticket on your desk: the analytics pipeline is too slow. It’s recalculating cumulative totals over a mutable dataset — millions of mixed reads and writes — and it’s crawling.
You know prefix sums. You learned them in your second year. Fast queries, but the moment any value changes, you’re rebuilding the whole table. You also know a plain array — fast updates, but then every query becomes a linear scan.
Both are useless here.
So you reach for the Segment Tree — the safe, powerful, 60-line answer your data structures course eventually got to. It’ll work. But a colleague glances over your shoulder and says, “Why not just use a Fenwick Tree? It’s faster, a tenth of the code, and half the memory.”
You nod slowly, pretending you know what that is.
This article is so you never have to do that again.

A Paper Written for Compression, Not Competitions
In 1994, a researcher named Peter Fenwick was working on arithmetic coding — a data compression technique that requires constantly looking up and updating cumulative symbol frequencies. His bottleneck was exactly the problem above: frequent updates and frequent prefix queries, neither of which a static table handles well.
His solution, published in Software: Practice and Experience (Vol. 24, №3), wasn’t designed to be elegant. It just turned out that way. He noticed something in the binary representations of array indices that nobody had exploited before — and that observation alone was enough to build a structure that does both operations in O(log n), using a single flat array, with no pointers, no recursion, and no tree nodes.
Competitive programmers later adopted it so heavily that it became a staple of coding contests. But its origin is worth remembering: this came from a real engineering problem, solved by someone paying close attention to bits.

The Thing Nobody Explains Properly
Every tutorial on Fenwick Trees will at some point show you this line:
i += i & (-i)And then say, “This moves to the next node.” Most stop there. That’s the moment most readers either memorize it blindly or give up.
Let’s actually understand it — because once you do, the whole structure becomes obvious.
Take any positive integer. Write it in binary. Find the rightmost 1 bit. That bit — and only that bit — is what i & (-i) extracts. It's called the lowest set bit, and in Fenwick's structure, it determines exactly how much of the array each index is responsible for.
Here’s the trick in slow motion:
i = 6 → binary: 0 1 1 0
-i (two's complement): 1 0 1 0
i & (-i): 0 0 1 0 = 2So lowbit(6) = 2. Index 6 is responsible for exactly 2 elements — elements 5 and 6.
i = 4 → binary: 0 1 0 0
-i: 1 1 0 0
i & (-i): 0 1 0 0 = 4lowbit(4) = 4. Index 4 owns 4 elements — elements 1 through 4.
i = 8 → binary: 1 0 0 0
-i: 1 0 0 0
i & (-i): 1 0 0 0 = 8lowbit(8) = 8. Index 8 owns everything — elements 1 through 8.
This isn’t arbitrary. Every index owns a chunk of the array whose size is exactly its lowest set bit. That design choice is what makes both operations fast. And now the two core operations become self-evident:
To query the prefix sum up to the index i: accumulate BIT[i], then jump to i - lowbit(i) (strip the lowest set bit, moving left). Repeat until you hit zero. You're collecting non-overlapping chunks that tile the range [1, i] exactly.
To update the index i by some delta: update BIT[i], then jump to i + lowbit(i) (add the lowest set bit, moving right toward higher-responsibility nodes). Repeat until you exceed n. You're propagating the change upward to every node whose range includes i.
For n = 8This is what the responsibilities look like:
Index: 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8
Owns: [1] [1,2] [3] [1-4] [5] [5,6] [7] [1-8]Query prefix sum up to 6: BIT[6] + BIT[4] → covers [5,6] + [1,4] = [1,6]. Two steps. Query prefix sum up to 7: BIT[7] + BIT[6] + BIT[4] → three steps.
The number of steps is bounded by the number of bits in n — which is ⌊log₂(n)⌋. That's your O(log n).

The Code (Now That It Makes Sense)
class FenwickTree:
def __init__(self, n: int):
self.n = n
self.tree = [0] * (n + 1) # 1-indexed; index 0 is unused def update(self, i: int, delta: int) -> None:
while i <= self.n:
self.tree[i] += delta
i += i & (-i) # move to next responsible ancestor def query(self, i: int) -> int:
total = 0
while i > 0:
total += self.tree[i]
i -= i & (-i) # strip lowest set bit, move left
return total def range_query(self, l: int, r: int) -> int:
return self.query(r) - self.query(l - 1)That’s it. update and query each is 3 lines of logic. The same in C++:
class FenwickTree {
int n;
vector<int> tree;
public:
FenwickTree(int n) : n(n), tree(n + 1, 0) {} void update(int i, int delta) {
for (; i <= n; i += i & (-i))
tree[i] += delta;
} int query(int i) {
int total = 0;
for (; i > 0; i -= i & (-i))
total += tree[i];
return total;
} int range_query(int l, int r) {
return query(r) - query(l - 1);
}
};One array. No heap allocation. No recursion. Every operation fits in cache. This is why, in practice, a Fenwick Tree is often faster than a Segment Tree even though both are theoretically O(log n) — constant factors matter, and pointer chasing doesn't.

Building in O(n), Not O(n log n)
The naive way to build a Fenwick Tree is to call update for each element — that's O(n log n). There's a smarter approach.
Every index i has a parent: i + lowbit(i). If you process indices left to right and push each node's value directly to its parent, you build the entire tree in a single pass:
def build(self, arr: list) -> None:
self.tree = [0] + arr[:] # copy into 1-indexed array
for i in range(1, self.n + 1):
parent = i + (i & (-i))
if parent <= self.n:
self.tree[parent] += self.tree[i]O(n). One pass, no redundant work. Use this whenever you're initializing from an existing array.

It Doesn’t Stop at 1D
The same structure extends naturally to two dimensions. Say you have a grid and need to:

• Add a value to the cell (x, y)

• Query the sum over any rectangle (x1, y1) to (x2, y2)
Next two BITs:
class FenwickTree2D:
def __init__(self, rows: int, cols: int):
self.rows = rows
self.cols = cols
self.tree = [[0] * (cols + 1) for _ in range(rows + 1)] def update(self, x: int, y: int, delta: int) -> None:
i = x
while i <= self.rows:
j = y
while j <= self.cols:
self.tree[i][j] += delta
j += j & (-j)
i += i & (-i) def query(self, x: int, y: int) -> int:
total = 0
i = x
while i > 0:
j = y
while j > 0:
total += self.tree[i][j]
j -= j & (-j)
i -= i & (-i)
return total def range_query(self, x1, y1, x2, y2) -> int:
return (self.query(x2, y2)
- self.query(x1 - 1, y2)
- self.query(x2, y1 - 1)
+ self.query(x1 - 1, y1 - 1))Complexity: O(log(rows) × log(cols)) per operation. This is genuinely useful in computational geometry, image processing, and spatial databases — anywhere you're aggregating over rectangular regions of mutable 2D data.

Flipping the Problem: Range Updates, Point Queries
Everything so far assumed point updates and range queries. What if you need the opposite — add a value to every element in a range [l, r], then read individual elements?
The classic trick: maintain a difference array inside the BIT.
def range_update(bit: FenwickTree, l: int, r: int, delta: int) -> None:
bit.update(l, delta)
bit.update(r + 1, -delta)def point_query(bit: FenwickTree, i: int) -> int:
return bit.query(i)range_update(l, r, delta) marks where the delta starts and cancels it after r. A prefix query at any position i accumulates exactly the deltas whose ranges cover i. Both operations stay O(log n).

The Fully General Case: Range Updates AND Range Queries
This is where most tutorials bail. Here’s the math and the code.
When you apply [l, r] += delta, the contribution to a prefix sum query at position p is:

• delta * (p - l + 1) if l ≤ p ≤ r

• delta * (r - l + 1) if p > r

• 0 if p < l
Working through the algebra, you need to maintain two BITs simultaneously:
class FenwickTreeRangeUpdate:
def __init__(self, n: int):
self.n = n
self.b1 = FenwickTree(n)
self.b2 = FenwickTree(n) def _update(self, i: int, delta: int) -> None:
self.b1.update(i, delta)
self.b2.update(i, delta * (i - 1)) def range_update(self, l: int, r: int, delta: int) -> None:
self._update(l, delta)
self._update(r + 1, -delta) def prefix_sum(self, i: int) -> int:
return self.b1.query(i) * i - self.b2.query(i) def range_query(self, l: int, r: int) -> int:
return self.prefix_sum(r) - self.prefix_sum(l - 1)All operations: O(log n). This matches a Segment Tree with lazy propagation — built with two arrays and less than 30 lines.

Where This Lives in the Real World
The Fenwick Tree isn’t just for competitive programming. It shows up in production systems under different names:
Arithmetic coding and data compression: Fenwick’s original use case. The structure is still used in LZMA and range coders for fast cumulative frequency lookups and updates.
Database query planning: PostgreSQL’s statistics collector maintains cumulative histograms over column values. The structure is BIT-equivalent — fast to update as rows are inserted or deleted, fast to query for planner estimates.
Inversion counting: Count pairs (i, j) where i < j but A[i] > A[j]. This is a proxy for "how unsorted is this array?" and appears in ranking algorithms, merge sort analysis, and sequence comparison. The BIT solution is O(n log n):
def count_inversions(arr):
n = len(arr)
sorted_unique = sorted(set(arr))
rank = {v: i + 1 for i, v in enumerate(sorted_unique)} bit = FenwickTree(n)
inversions = 0
for idx, val in enumerate(arr):
r = rank[val]
inversions += idx - bit.query(r) # elements seen so far, greater than val
bit.update(r, 1)
return inversions
Fenwick vs. Segment Tree: The Honest Comparison
People treat this as a religious debate. It isn’t.
Fenwick Tree Segment Tree Code length ~10 lines ~50–60 lines Memory n ~4n Cache behavior Excellent (flat array) Worse (index arithmetic or pointer jumps) Supported operations Invertible only (sum, XOR, product) Anything (min, max, GCD, custom merge) Range updates Two BITs + algebra Lazy propagation 2D Natural Verbose
If you need min or max queries, you can’t use a Fenwick Tree — the inverse operation doesn’t exist for those. Use a Segment Tree.
For everything else — sum, XOR, product — the Fenwick Tree is faster in practice, simpler to implement, and less likely to have a subtle off-by-one bug at 2 AM before a contest.

Complexity Reference
Operation Time Space Build (naive) O(n log n) O(n) Build (optimized) O(n) O(n) Point update O(log n) — Prefix/range query O(log n) — Range update + point query O(log n) O(n) Range update + range query O(log n) O(n) (×2) 2D point update O(log n · log m) O(nm) 2D range query O(log n · log m) —

Read the Original Paper
Peter Fenwick, “A New Data Structure for Cumulative Frequency Tables,” Software: Practice and Experience, Vol. 24, №3, 1994. It’s 7 pages. The notation is dated, but the reasoning is clean, and you’ll walk away understanding exactly why the bit manipulation works — not just that it does.

What’s Next
The natural follow-up question after learning the Fenwick Tree is: “If this handles sums so cleanly, why does the Segment Tree even exist?”
The answer is that there’s a whole class of problems — range minimum, range maximum, custom interval merging — where you can’t undo operations, so the BIT’s inverse trick breaks down. The Segment Tree handles these with lazy propagation, a mechanism that defers work until it’s actually needed.
That’s Article 2. And once you’ve seen how lazy propagation works, you’ll understand why it’s the gateway to persistent data structures, functional trees, and eventually link-cut trees — some of the most powerful tools in the algorithmic toolkit.

Part of the Advanced Algorithms & Data Structures series — tracing the tools used in real systems bac
